context:
Section 8 (Conclusions and Future Work), page 35

Second, it would be interesting to understand whether Scarf’s algorithm converges in polynomial time on the bipartite matching polytope for any ordinal matrix C. This would require an understanding of the problem that goes beyond stable marriages.
title: Polynomial-time convergence of Scarf’s algorithm on the bipartite matching polytope for any ordinal matrix \(C\)
background_latex:
Let \(G=(M\cup W,E)\) be a bipartite graph with \(|M|=|W|=k\). Let \(n:=|M|+|W|=2k\) and let \(E\) index \(m:=|E|\) edge-variables \(x\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}\). Let \(A'\in\{0,1\}^{n\times m}\) be the node-edge incidence matrix of \(G\), and define the standard-form matrix \(A=(I\mid A')\in\mathbb{Q}^{n\times (n+m)}_{\ge 0}\), where \(I\) is the \(n\times n\) identity matrix (corresponding to \(n\) slack/loop variables) and \(b:=\mathbf{1}\in\mathbb{Q}^n_{>0}\). The associated down-monotone polytope is \(P:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n+m}_{\ge 0}: Ax=b\}\), which corresponds to the (bipartite) matching polytope in this formulation.

Let \(C\in\mathbb{Z}^{n\times(n+m)}\) be an ordinal matrix, meaning that all entries are distinct and for each row \(i\in[n]\) and all \(j\ne i\) and all slack/loop columns \(k\in[n+m]\setminus[n]\), the inequalities \(c_{i,i}<c_{i,k}<c_{i,j}\) hold. Scarf’s algorithm is a pivoting procedure that, given \((A,b,C)\) and starting from an initial feasible basis, iteratively performs:
- a cardinal pivot that updates a feasible basis \(B\) for \(Ax=b\), and
- an ordinal pivot that updates an ordinal basis \(D\) for \(C\),
maintaining \(|B\cap D|\ge n-1\), and halting when \(B=D\), yielding a dominating basis (and hence a dominating vertex of \(P\)).

For a pivoting execution, let \(T\) be the number of iterations (cardinal+ordinal pivot pairs) until termination.
problem_definition_latex: Show that for every bipartite graph \(G=(M\cup W,E)\) with \(|M|=|W|\), for \(A=(I\mid A')\) and \(b=\mathbf{1}\) as defined above, and for every ordinal matrix \(C\in\mathbb{Z}^{n\times(n+m)}\), Scarf’s algorithm (with its uniquely defined ordinal pivots and some well-specified rule for resolving degeneracy in cardinal pivots, if needed) always terminates after \(T\le \mathrm{poly}(n,m)\) iterations.
latex:
Let \(G=(M\cup W,E)\) be a bipartite graph with \(|M|=|W|=k\). Let \(n:=|M|+|W|=2k\) and let \(E\) index \(m:=|E|\) edge-variables \(x\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}\). Let \(A'\in\{0,1\}^{n\times m}\) be the node-edge incidence matrix of \(G\), and define the standard-form matrix \(A=(I\mid A')\in\mathbb{Q}^{n\times (n+m)}_{\ge 0}\), where \(I\) is the \(n\times n\) identity matrix (corresponding to \(n\) slack/loop variables) and \(b:=\mathbf{1}\in\mathbb{Q}^n_{>0}\). The associated down-monotone polytope is \(P:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n+m}_{\ge 0}: Ax=b\}\), which corresponds to the (bipartite) matching polytope in this formulation.

Let \(C\in\mathbb{Z}^{n\times(n+m)}\) be an ordinal matrix, meaning that all entries are distinct and for each row \(i\in[n]\) and all \(j\ne i\) and all slack/loop columns \(k\in[n+m]\setminus[n]\), the inequalities \(c_{i,i}<c_{i,k}<c_{i,j}\) hold. Scarf’s algorithm is a pivoting procedure that, given \((A,b,C)\) and starting from an initial feasible basis, iteratively performs:
- a cardinal pivot that updates a feasible basis \(B\) for \(Ax=b\), and
- an ordinal pivot that updates an ordinal basis \(D\) for \(C\),
maintaining \(|B\cap D|\ge n-1\), and halting when \(B=D\), yielding a dominating basis (and hence a dominating vertex of \(P\)).

For a pivoting execution, let \(T\) be the number of iterations (cardinal+ordinal pivot pairs) until termination.

Show that for every bipartite graph \(G=(M\cup W,E)\) with \(|M|=|W|\), for \(A=(I\mid A')\) and \(b=\mathbf{1}\) as defined above, and for every ordinal matrix \(C\in\mathbb{Z}^{n\times(n+m)}\), Scarf’s algorithm (with its uniquely defined ordinal pivots and some well-specified rule for resolving degeneracy in cardinal pivots, if needed) always terminates after \(T\le \mathrm{poly}(n,m)\) iterations.
